heroOverview: Cystinuria is an autosomal recessive disorder of renal amino acid transport in which the proximal tubule fails to reabsorb cystine, ornithine, lysine, and arginine from the glomerular filtrate. Because cystine is the least soluble of all naturally occurring amino acids, its overexcretion leads to crystal formation and stone disease in the collecting system of the kidney, ureter, and bladder. Cystinuria causes approximately 1–2% of all kidney stones in adults and up to 8–10% of kidney stones in children, making it the most important monogenic cause of nephrolithiasis. The condition affects an estimated 1 in 7,000 live births worldwide and carries a high stone recurrence rate — most patients pass or require treatment for stones multiple times throughout their lives. Despite this burden, no cure currently exists and management centres on high fluid intake, urinary alkalinisation, and pharmacological treatment to increase cystine solubility. Untreated or poorly managed cystinuria can lead to chronic kidney disease through repeated surgical trauma and stone obstruction.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 7,000 live births globally; affects around 20,000 people in the United States and 6,000 in the United Kingdom
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally in terms of inheritance; males tend to have a more severe clinical course with earlier and more frequent stone episodes due to larger urinary cystine output per body mass
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at first stone episode is approximately 12–14 years, but range spans from infancy to sixth decade; stones typically recur throughout life
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1 million people affected worldwide; the most common monogenic cause of kidney stones

definition: Cystinuria (ICD-10: E72.01) is a disorder of dibasic amino acid transport in the proximal renal tubule and intestinal epithelium, caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in either the SLC3A1 gene (encoding the heavy subunit rBAT) or the SLC7A9 gene (encoding the light subunit b0,+AT) of the renal epithelial transporter responsible for reabsorbing cystine, ornithine, lysine, and arginine. The genetic classification divides cystinuria into type A (biallelic SLC3A1 mutations, more severe; formerly type I) and type B (biallelic SLC7A9 mutations; formerly types II and III). Carriers of type A (SLC3A1 heterozygotes) excrete normal amounts of cystine and do not form stones; carriers of type B (SLC7A9 heterozygotes) excrete moderately elevated cystine and rarely form stones. The clinical consequence of the transport defect is specific to cystine because the other dibasic amino acids — ornithine, lysine, and arginine — remain soluble at physiological urinary pH and concentration. Cystine, in contrast, precipitates at concentrations above approximately 243 mg per litre at pH 7.0, and urinary cystine in homozygotes typically exceeds 600–1,200 mg per day, far above the threshold for crystallisation. Cystine stones are characteristically yellowish-white, radio-opaque on plain X-ray (though less so than calcium oxalate), and tend to form staghorn calculi that fill the renal collecting system. The condition falls primarily under urology and nephrology, with metabolic medicine and endocrinology involved for biochemical management.

typesClassification.description: Caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC3A1, encoding the rBAT glycoprotein anchor of the transporter. Heterozygotes excrete normal urinary cystine. Homozygotes have severe cystine overexcretion (often >800 mg/day) and the most aggressive stone disease.

Caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC7A9, encoding the catalytic subunit. Heterozygotes excrete moderately elevated cystine (intermediate phenotype) and rarely form stones. Homozygotes have cystine overexcretion similar in magnitude to type A.
Compound heterozygosity for one SLC3A1 and one SLC7A9 pathogenic variant; phenotype is similar to homozygous forms. Represents the rarer double-heterozygous scenario.

primarySymptoms: Renal colic — severe, sudden-onset loin-to-groin pain occurring when a cystine stone moves from the kidney into the ureter, the most characteristic and disabling symptom of cystinuria.
Haematuria — visible blood in the urine (gross haematuria) or microscopic blood on urinalysis, caused by stone movement or mucosal injury in the urinary collecting system.
Recurrent urinary tract infections, particularly in the setting of obstructing or staghorn cystine calculi that harbour bacteria and prevent complete antibiotic sterilisation.
Urinary obstruction — incomplete or complete blockage of urine flow by a stone lodged in the ureter, causing hydronephrosis that may be asymptomatic or produce flank pain and reduced kidney function.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying renal colic, severe enough in some patients to prevent oral fluid intake and worsen stone risk through dehydration.
Bilateral flank pain in patients with bilateral renal calculi or staghorn stones involving both kidneys simultaneously.
Reduced urine output or anuria in rare cases of bilateral ureteric obstruction by stones — a urological emergency.
Characteristic hexagonal crystals visible on urine microscopy, which are pathognomonic for cystinuria and may be the first diagnostic finding on urinalysis performed for any reason.
Chronic kidney disease developing insidiously in patients with repeated stone episodes, surgical procedures, and recurrent obstruction causing cumulative nephron loss.
Growth impairment and delayed puberty in children with frequent stone episodes and repeated hospitalisations affecting nutritional intake and general health.
earlyWarningSigns: Hexagonal crystals on urine microscopy — their presence is essentially diagnostic of cystinuria and should prompt plasma and quantitative urine amino acid analysis.
An unexplained radio-opaque renal stone in a child or teenager, which should always prompt biochemical investigation including urine nitroprusside test and quantitative urine cystine.
A first episode of renal colic in a person under 30 years of age, which has a higher prior probability of a metabolic cause such as cystinuria than in an older patient.
A family history of kidney stones in a first-degree relative, particularly in childhood or young adulthood.
emergencySigns: Complete ureteric obstruction causing no urine output (anuria) — a urological emergency requiring urgent decompression by ureteroscopy or nephrostomy.
Obstructing ureteric stone with fever and rigors suggesting infection proximal to the obstruction (obstructive pyelonephritis) — requires emergency decompression within hours alongside antibiotics.
Bilateral obstructing stones causing acute kidney injury with rapid rise in serum creatinine.
Severe renal colic with persistent vomiting preventing oral fluid intake and oral analgesics, requiring intravenous hydration and pain control.
Urosepsis from an infected cystine calculus — confusion, high fever, low blood pressure, and tachycardia requiring emergency hospital admission.

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in both copies of SLC3A1 abolish the rBAT protein that anchors the cystine transporter complex to the renal proximal tubule brush border, eliminating cystine reabsorption and causing overexcretion exceeding 800–1,200 mg per day in homozygotes.

Pathogenic variants in both SLC7A9 alleles disrupt the catalytic b0,+AT transporter subunit responsible for the actual amino acid exchange at the proximal tubule membrane, producing a similar degree of cystinuria in homozygotes to type A.

Cystine's low solubility threshold of approximately 243 mg/L at neutral pH means that even moderate overexcretion creates persistently supersaturated urine that precipitates into hexagonal crystals, aggregates into calculi, and can rapidly form staghorn stones that fill the entire renal pelvis.

Cystine is even less soluble in acidic urine; at pH 5.0 the solubility threshold falls to approximately 150–170 mg/L. Most people's urine is naturally acidic for several hours each day — particularly during sleep — making nocturnal stone formation a particular risk in cystinuria.

Concentrating urine elevates cystine concentration proportionally. Physical activity, hot climates, illness with vomiting, and low habitual fluid intake all reduce urine output, raising cystine concentration above the crystallisation threshold and accelerating stone formation.

The same transporter is expressed in small intestinal epithelium; its deficiency in cystinuria mildly impairs intestinal cystine absorption, which reduces cystine availability but does not compensate for the urinary loss and is not clinically significant from a nutritional standpoint given that cysteine can be synthesised from methionine.

riskFactors.description: Homozygosity or compound heterozygosity for pathogenic variants in either gene is necessary for the full stone-forming phenotype. Disease prevalence is approximately 1 in 7,000 worldwide, consistent with a carrier frequency of roughly 1 in 42.

Although inheritance is equally distributed between sexes, males have an earlier age of stone onset, higher frequency of stone events, and greater risk of renal function impairment, possibly due to hormonal differences in cystine excretion or lower urine volume per kilogram body mass.

Achieving a urine output below 3 litres per day — particularly below 2 litres — dramatically increases cystine concentration and crystallisation risk; fluid intake is the single most modifiable determinant of stone recurrence in cystinuria.

Dietary sodium increases renal cystine excretion by reducing proximal tubule reabsorption; a low-sodium diet (below 2 g per day) can reduce urinary cystine excretion by 20–30% and is a first-line dietary recommendation.

Methionine is the dietary precursor of cysteine and cystine; very high protein diets containing large amounts of methionine-rich foods (red meat, eggs) increase urinary cystine output by approximately 15–20% and should be moderated.
Habitual low urine pH — common in high-meat diets and after prolonged sleep — increases supersaturation; urinary alkalinisation to pH 7.0–7.5 more than doubles effective cystine solubility.

riskFactors.factor: Hot climate or prolonged physical activity
riskFactors.description: Environmental or exercise-related dehydration reduces urine volume and concentrates cystine; patients living in tropical or desert climates or engaging in endurance sports require proportionally higher fluid targets.

Cystinuria management requires high daily fluid volumes, regular urine pH monitoring, and often multiple medications taken several times daily; adherence drops substantially in adolescence and young adulthood, a period when stone recurrence is highest.

affectedDemographics: Children and teenagers — cystinuria accounts for approximately 8–10% of kidney stones in the paediatric population
Young adults, particularly males, who have their first stone episode in the second or third decade of life
Adults of any age with a genetically confirmed diagnosis managed for recurrent stone disease
Individuals from populations where the SLC3A1 Libyan Jewish founder mutation or other regional founder variants elevate carrier frequency
Patients with chronic kidney disease resulting from years of obstructive and surgical kidney trauma
diagnosisOverview: Cystinuria is suspected when a patient presents with renal colic and hexagonal crystals are found on urine microscopy — a finding that is essentially pathognomonic. The urine nitroprusside (cyanide-nitroprusside) test provides a simple colorimetric screen: cystine above approximately 75–125 mg per gram of creatinine produces a red-purple colour, though false-negatives occur at lower concentrations. Quantitative 24-hour urine cystine measurement is the gold-standard biochemical test; an output above 400 mg per day in adults (above 75 mg per gram creatinine in children) in the context of cystine stones confirms the diagnosis. Plasma amino acid analysis may show mildly elevated cystine, ornithine, lysine, and arginine but can be normal. Genetic testing of the SLC3A1 and SLC7A9 genes identifies the specific mutations, classifies the type, predicts carrier status for family members, and guides prognosis. Stone analysis by infrared spectroscopy or X-ray crystallography confirms that stones are composed of cystine — characteristically yellowish-white, waxy in appearance, and faintly radio-opaque on plain X-ray, though CT urography without contrast is the most sensitive imaging modality for detection. Initial imaging in acute colic should be CT KUB (kidneys, ureters, bladder) without contrast, which detects cystine stones reliably. Renal ultrasound is used for follow-up to avoid cumulative radiation in young patients monitored over decades. Differential diagnosis includes other causes of nephrolithiasis (calcium oxalate, uric acid, struvite, calcium phosphate stones), all of which are distinguished by stone analysis and urine biochemistry.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathognomonic hexagonal cystine crystals in fresh urine; their presence essentially confirms cystinuria without further testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh midstream urine sample examined under a microscope; the hexagonal crystal morphology is distinctive and visually identifiable by an experienced laboratory technologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive biochemical test; confirms diagnosis and quantifies severity; guides management targets (aim to reduce urine cystine to below 250 mg/day or below 1,000 mg/L concentration).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: All urine collected for a full 24-hour period in a provided container; the sample is refrigerated or acidified during collection. Results available in 2–5 business days from a specialist metabolic laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A simple qualitative screen for cystine overexcretion; produces a red-purple colour reaction in positive specimens. Used as a bedside or general laboratory screen before quantitative testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A few drops of urine are mixed with reagents; colour change is read within minutes. A positive result prompts quantitative testing. False-negatives occur below 75 mg/g creatinine.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive imaging modality for detecting and characterising renal and ureteral stones including cystine; defines stone burden, obstruction, and renal anatomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis without intravenous contrast, taking 5–10 minutes; the patient lies still. Involves ionising radiation; renal ultrasound is preferred for follow-up monitoring in young patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the stone as cystine, which guides diagnosis and long-term management; cystine stones appear yellowish-white, waxy, and smooth.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Any passed or surgically retrieved stone fragment is sent to a specialist laboratory; results in 1–2 weeks. Patients should always save passed stones or stone fragments for analysis.

diagnosticTests.test: SLC3A1 and SLC7A9 molecular genetic testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific causative mutations, determines the cystinuria type, enables family carrier testing, and provides prognostic information about likely stone severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory; results in 3–6 weeks. Genetic counselling should accompany result disclosure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses baseline and ongoing kidney function in patients with repeated stone episodes or chronic obstruction, as cumulative renal injury from cystinuria can lead to chronic kidney disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood sample; results within hours. Monitored annually in all patients and more frequently when stone burden or treatment intensity is high.

treatmentOverview: Management of cystinuria is lifelong and centres on three strategies that together reduce the urinary cystine concentration below the crystallisation threshold of approximately 250 mg per litre. The first and most important is aggressive hydration: patients are recommended to drink at least 3–4 litres of fluid per day, targeting a urine output above 3 litres per day, spread continuously throughout 24 hours including at least one overnight void. The second strategy is urinary alkalinisation: increasing urine pH from the normal range of 5–6 to 7.0–7.5 more than doubles cystine solubility. Potassium citrate is preferred because sodium bicarbonate raises urinary sodium, which paradoxically increases cystine excretion. Dietary modification — reduced sodium (below 2 g per day) and moderate protein restriction targeting methionine sources — complements pharmacological management. When hydration and alkalinisation alone are insufficient, pharmacological thiol therapy is added: D-penicillamine and tiopronin (alpha-mercaptopropionylglycine) each form a mixed disulfide with cysteine that is approximately 50 times more soluble than cystine, effectively increasing the urinary carrying capacity. Captopril, an ACE inhibitor with a free thiol group, has been used in some centres as a better-tolerated alternative, though evidence is weaker. Acute stone episodes are managed with analgesia, hydration, and, depending on stone size and position, ureteroscopy, shockwave lithotripsy (SWL), or percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) — SWL is less effective for cystine stones than for calcium oxalate stones and is not first-line. Lifelong follow-up with 24-hour urine cystine monitoring, periodic imaging, and renal function assessment is essential.

medicalTreatments.name: High fluid intake (first-line, cornerstone)
medicalTreatments.description: Drinking 3–4 litres of fluid daily — targeting over 3 litres of urine output per 24 hours — is the single most important treatment. Fluids are distributed continuously including overnight, with patients waking once to drink and void. Water is the primary recommended fluid; some centres advocate alkaline mineral waters.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained high fluid intake reduces stone recurrence by approximately 40–50% compared with lower intake; compliance is the primary limiting factor in long-term efficacy.

medicalTreatments.name: Urinary alkalinisation with potassium citrate
medicalTreatments.description: Oral potassium citrate raises urine pH to 7.0–7.5, more than doubling the solubility of cystine. Typical doses are 30–80 mEq per day in 2–3 divided doses, titrated using urine pH strips or a pH meter. Potassium citrate is preferred over sodium bicarbonate because the latter increases sodium load and worsens cystine excretion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Alkalinisation to pH 7.0–7.5 increases effective cystine solubility by more than 2-fold; combined with fluid therapy, achieves stone-free status in approximately 50–60% of compliant patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Tiopronin (alpha-mercaptopropionylglycine, Thiola)
medicalTreatments.description: A thiol drug that reacts with urinary cystine to form a soluble mixed disulfide up to 50 times more soluble than cystine itself, substantially reducing free cystine available for crystallisation. Taken orally 2–3 times daily. Better tolerated than D-penicillamine with fewer serious adverse effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urinary free cystine by 50–70% in patients taking an effective dose; 24-hour urine monitoring guides dose titration. Achieves stone-free status in an additional 30–40% of patients failing diet and alkalinisation alone.

medicalTreatments.name: D-penicillamine
medicalTreatments.description: The first thiol drug used for cystinuria, forming a highly soluble mixed disulfide with cysteine. Effective but has a higher rate of significant adverse effects including autoimmune nephropathy, haematological suppression, and dermatological reactions, making monitoring mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urinary cystine by up to 50–75% in adherent patients but is now second-line to tiopronin in most guidelines due to its side-effect profile.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-sodium, moderate-protein diet
medicalTreatments.description: Reducing dietary sodium to below 2 g per day lowers renal cystine excretion by 20–30% by reducing proximal tubule solute load. Limiting methionine-rich foods (red meat, eggs, chicken) to moderate quantities reduces the precursor supply for cystine synthesis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dietary sodium restriction alone reduces 24-hour urinary cystine by approximately 20–30 mg per gram reduction in daily sodium intake, as demonstrated in controlled dietary studies.

surgicalOptions.name: Ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy
surgicalOptions.description: A flexible ureteroscope is passed up the ureter under general or spinal anaesthesia; a holmium laser fragments the cystine stone into small pieces that are extracted or passed. Now the preferred intervention for most ureteral and small-to-medium renal cystine stones.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of approximately 70–85% for ureteral stones and 50–70% for renal stones under 2 cm; often requires multiple sessions for larger stone burden.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
surgicalOptions.description: A nephroscope is placed directly into the renal collecting system through the skin of the flank under imaging guidance; larger stones and staghorn cystine calculi are fragmented by laser or ultrasonic lithotripsy and extracted. First-line for staghorn or stones above 2 cm.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of 70–90% for large renal stones in experienced centres; may require staged procedures for very large or branched staghorn calculi.

surgicalOptions.name: Shockwave lithotripsy (SWL)
surgicalOptions.description: External focused shockwaves fragment stones through skin; less effective for cystine stones than for calcium oxalate because cystine has a harder, more elastic matrix that resists fragmentation. Used for small stones less than 10 mm as a less invasive option when patients prefer to avoid ureteroscopy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of approximately 40–60% for cystine stones less than 10 mm, compared with 75–90% for equivalent calcium oxalate stones; multiple sessions often required.

alternativeTreatments.name: Alkalising mineral water
alternativeTreatments.description: Some high-bicarbonate mineral waters (e.g. with pH above 8.0) have been used as a dietary adjunct to potassium citrate for alkalinisation, as patients find them more palatable than citrate supplements; efficacy data are limited but they are a reasonable adjunct to standard therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Some early reports suggested pyridoxine reduces cystine excretion, possibly by modifying amino acid metabolism; evidence is weak and pyridoxine is not part of standard cystinuria guidelines, but it has a good safety profile at recommended doses.

whySeeSpecialist: All confirmed or suspected cystinuria patients should be managed by a multidisciplinary team including a urologist with experience in endourology (ureteroscopy and PCNL), a nephrologist or metabolic physician, and a specialist metabolic dietitian. The complexity of optimising fluid targets, urinary alkalinisation, and thiol therapy — particularly during pregnancy, childhood, and in the setting of declining renal function — requires specialist expertise. The EAU (European Association of Urology) and AUA (American Urological Association) guidelines both specifically recommend specialist management for cystinuria.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a urologist with specific experience in paediatric or complex recurrent stone disease, including competence in flexible ureteroscopy and PCNL, ideally within a centre that also has a metabolic stone clinic. The metabolic component — 24-hour urine monitoring, urinary pH targets, thiol drug prescribing — is best co-managed by a nephrologist or metabolic physician who routinely prescribes tiopronin and potassium citrate and reviews 24-hour urine results. A specialist dietitian familiar with the low-sodium, moderate-protein recommendations for cystinuria is valuable, particularly in paediatric cases.
preventionStrategies: Drink at least 3–4 litres of fluid daily, distributed across the full 24 hours including at least one overnight void, to maintain urine output above 3 litres per day and keep cystine below the crystallisation threshold.
Monitor urine pH at home using pH paper or a pH meter; target a consistent range of 7.0–7.5 by adjusting potassium citrate dose.
Follow a low-sodium diet (below 2 g per day) and eat animal protein in moderation to reduce renal cystine excretion.
Take prescribed thiol therapy (tiopronin or D-penicillamine) consistently if biochemical targets cannot be met with fluids and alkalinisation alone.
Attend 24-hour urine cystine monitoring every 6–12 months to confirm that biochemical targets are being met and adjust treatment accordingly.
Undergo renal imaging (ultrasound) every 1–2 years to detect new stone formation before stones become symptomatic or cause silent obstruction.
Seek genetic counselling and offer family member testing so that affected siblings can begin preventive treatment before stone formation.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a reusable water bottle at all times and develop a habit of drinking regularly throughout the day rather than in response to thirst alone.
Set a phone alarm to drink and void during the night to prevent cystine concentration rising during prolonged sleep.
Reduce table salt use, processed food consumption, and restaurant meals, as these are the primary sources of dietary sodium.
Reduce red meat, eggs, and poultry to moderate servings (one per day) rather than high-protein meals at every sitting.
Keep pH strips accessible at home and test urine pH at least twice daily while on alkalinisation therapy, adjusting potassium citrate accordingly.
Save any passed stone or stone fragment in a sealed container for stone analysis at the metabolic clinic.

recommended: Water and other calorie-free fluids in large quantities (3–4 litres per day); alkaline mineral waters may serve as a partial alkalinisation adjunct
Fruits and vegetables, which provide citrate and potassium that assist urinary alkalinisation
Low-sodium foods — fresh produce, unprocessed grains, and home-prepared meals with minimal added salt
Moderate dairy intake for calcium, as adequate dietary calcium binds intestinal oxalate and does not worsen cystinuria; overly low calcium intake is not beneficial
avoid: High-sodium processed foods, cured meats, salty snacks, fast food, and restaurant meals — sodium drives renal cystine excretion
Very high protein meals heavy in methionine: large servings of red meat, eggs, and shellfish at every meal
Acid-forming drinks consumed in excess such as cranberry juice, which lowers urine pH and worsens cystine solubility
Excessive ascorbic acid (vitamin C) supplementation, which acidifies urine and reduces cystine solubility

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is beneficial for general health and does not need to be restricted in cystinuria. Endurance exercise, particularly in warm conditions, carries a risk of dehydration that concentrates urine and increases stone risk. Athletes and those exercising vigorously should increase fluid intake proportionally before, during, and after activity — target pre-exercise hydration of an additional 500–750 mL and replace losses during exercise. Contact sports are not contraindicated, though a clinician should review stone burden before participation in activities with a significant risk of renal trauma.
dailyManagement: Track daily fluid intake using a measured bottle or app to ensure the 3–4 litre target is reached consistently, not just on some days.
Test urine pH morning and evening with pH strips; record results and adjust potassium citrate dose in consultation with the metabolic team.
Take tiopronin or D-penicillamine at the prescribed times with adequate fluid; do not skip doses during illness or travel.
Report any new flank pain, blood in urine, fever, or reduced urine output to the metabolic team immediately rather than waiting for a scheduled visit.
Keep an emergency urology contact number available; stone obstruction with infection requires same-day decompression.
prognosis: Cystinuria is a lifelong condition that cannot be cured, but with consistent management most patients maintain adequate renal function and acceptable quality of life. The main determinants of long-term outcome are the frequency and severity of stone episodes, the degree of surgical intervention required, and adherence to preventive therapy. Approximately 70% of patients have at least one stone episode requiring intervention by the age of 40. About 25–30% of cystinuria patients develop some degree of chronic kidney disease over their lifetime, primarily from repeated obstruction and surgical trauma rather than from the amino acid transport defect itself. End-stage renal disease is uncommon but occurs in approximately 1–2% of patients. Male sex, bilateral staghorn stones, early age of onset, and high baseline cystine excretion are associated with worse renal outcomes. Adherence to high fluid intake, urinary alkalinisation, and appropriate use of thiol therapy significantly improves the stone recurrence rate and preserves renal function over time.
recoveryTimeline: After an acute stone episode — whether passed spontaneously or treated endoscopically — recovery from pain and haematuria takes one to seven days. Return to normal activity after ureteroscopy is typically two to five days; after PCNL it is one to three weeks. The prevention of the next stone episode is the primary ongoing goal; with good biochemical control (urine cystine below 250 mg/L, pH 7.0–7.5), stone formation rates in compliant patients can be reduced to fewer than one episode every two to three years compared with multiple episodes annually without treatment.
complications: Chronic kidney disease — the most serious long-term complication, developing in approximately 25–30% of patients from repeated obstructive episodes and surgical interventions; closely linked to treatment adherence.
Staghorn calculi — large branched cystine stones filling the entire renal pelvis, requiring complex PCNL; carry high risk of renal function impairment and post-operative infection.
Urosepsis — life-threatening infection arising from infected cystine stones proximal to obstruction; requires emergency decompression and intensive antimicrobial treatment.
End-stage renal failure — affects approximately 1–2% of cystinuria patients in long-term follow-up cohort data; necessitates dialysis or kidney transplantation.
Psychological burden and quality-of-life impairment from unpredictable, severely painful stone episodes and the demands of complex preventive regimens affecting diet, sleep, and social activities.
Surgical complications from repeated ureteroscopy, PCNL, or SWL procedures including ureteral stricture, bleeding, and anaesthetic risks that accumulate over a lifetime of recurrent stone disease.

supportResources.name: Rare Kidney Stone Consortium — Cystinuria
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician resource from the US Rare Kidney Stone Consortium, with information on cystinuria diagnosis, management, and clinical trials.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Kidney Stones in Adults
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information on kidney stones including cystine stones from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: National Organization for Rare Disorders (NORD) — Cystinuria
supportResources.description: Detailed patient-facing summary of cystinuria including symptoms, causes, inheritance, and treatment options.

sources.title: Palacin M, Goodyer P, Nunes V, Gasparini P. Cystinuria. Chapter in The Metabolic and Molecular Bases of Inherited Disease (8th ed.)
Bhatt NR, Bhatt S, Parikh V et al. Cystinuria: diagnosis and management. Reviews in Urology, 2021
Pearle MS, Goldfarb DS, Assimos DG et al. Medical management of kidney stones: AUA guideline, 2014
EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2023 — European Association of Urology
Servais A, Thomas K, Dello Strologo L et al. Cystinuria: clinical practice recommendation. Kidney International, 2021

confusedWithConditions.name: Calcium oxalate kidney stones
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Calcium oxalate stones are the most common type (80% of stones) and have many causes; cystine stones are specific to cystinuria, recur more frequently, are less amenable to shockwave lithotripsy, and require distinct biochemical management targeting urinary cystine rather than oxalate or calcium.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cystinosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cystinosis is a lysosomal storage disorder causing intracellular cystine accumulation that damages multiple organs including the kidneys, eyes, and thyroid; cystinuria is a renal transport defect causing urinary cystine overexcretion and stone disease without intracellular accumulation or multi-organ damage.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal tubular acidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Renal tubular acidosis causes acidic urine from impaired proton secretion and can increase stone risk from calcium phosphate deposition; it does not cause cystine overexcretion and is diagnosed by different urine chemistry findings.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney stones (general)
relatedConditions.relevance: Cystinuria is the most important monogenic cause of kidney stones; cystine stone management overlaps significantly with general stone prevention principles of hydration and dietary modification.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrolithiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader clinical category that cystinuria stone disease belongs to; epidemiological and management data for nephrolithiasis provide the comparative context for cystinuria's burden.

relatedConditions.name: Cystinosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A distinct disorder involving cystine but through a completely different mechanism; commonly confused with cystinuria by patients and non-specialist clinicians.

relatedConditions.name: Disorders of amino acid transport (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader ICD category for renal amino acid transport defects; Fanconi syndrome and Hartnup disease are related transport disorders in the same metabolic grouping.

faqs.question: What is cystinuria?
faqs.answer: Cystinuria is an inherited disorder where the kidneys cannot properly reabsorb the amino acid cystine, causing it to accumulate in urine. Because cystine dissolves poorly, it crystallises and forms kidney stones. It accounts for roughly 1–2% of adult kidney stones and up to 10% of kidney stones in children.

faqs.question: What causes cystine kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Cystine stones are caused by mutations in the SLC3A1 or SLC7A9 genes, which encode a transporter in the kidney tubule responsible for reabsorbing cystine back into the blood. Without this transporter, cystine spills into urine at high concentrations, crystallises, and forms stones.

faqs.question: Is cystinuria hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cystinuria is autosomal recessive: a child must inherit a faulty gene copy from both parents to be affected. Both parents are carriers but are usually asymptomatic. Each pregnancy of two carriers has a 25% chance of producing an affected child.

faqs.question: How is cystinuria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Cystinuria is suspected when hexagonal crystals appear on urine microscopy or when a young person has recurrent kidney stones. A 24-hour urine cystine collection confirms excessive excretion (above 400 mg/day in adults). A urine nitroprusside test provides a rapid screen. Genetic testing identifies the specific mutations.

faqs.question: How is cystinuria treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets three goals: keeping urine volume above 3 litres per day to dilute cystine, raising urine pH to 7.0–7.5 with potassium citrate to improve cystine solubility, and reducing dietary sodium. If these measures are insufficient, tiopronin or D-penicillamine are added to chemically bind cystine into a more soluble form.

faqs.question: How much water should I drink if I have cystinuria?
faqs.answer: At least 3–4 litres of fluid daily — enough to produce over 3 litres of urine per 24 hours. Fluids must be spread throughout the day and night. Drinking a large glass of water before bed and once overnight is important because cystine concentrates in urine during sleep.

faqs.question: Can cystinuria cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes, in some patients. Repeated stone episodes, surgical interventions, and prolonged obstruction can cause cumulative kidney damage. About 25–30% of cystinuria patients develop some degree of chronic kidney disease over their lifetime, and around 1–2% progress to end-stage renal failure requiring dialysis or transplant.

faqs.question: What does cystinuria have to do with diet?
faqs.answer: Diet affects cystine excretion. A low-sodium diet (below 2 g per day) reduces renal cystine output by 20–30%. Moderating methionine-rich foods (red meat, eggs) further reduces cystine production. Avoiding acid-producing drinks and vitamin C supplements helps keep urine pH in the target alkaline range.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cystinuria and cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Despite the similar names, these are completely different conditions. Cystinuria is a renal transport defect causing high urinary cystine and kidney stones. Cystinosis is a lysosomal storage disorder causing cystine to accumulate inside cells, damaging the kidneys, eyes, thyroid, and muscles. They require entirely different management.

faqs.question: Can cystinuria stones pass on their own?
faqs.answer: Small cystine stones (under about 5 mm) may pass spontaneously with high fluid intake, but cystine stones are harder and less likely to pass without intervention than calcium oxalate stones of the same size. Stones above 6–7 mm rarely pass without urological intervention.

faqs.question: What surgery is used for cystine stones?
faqs.answer: Ureteroscopy with laser fragmentation is the preferred treatment for most cystine stones. Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) is used for large or staghorn cystine stones. Shockwave lithotripsy is less effective for cystine than for other stone types and is only considered for small stones when less invasive options are preferred.

faqs.question: What is tiopronin and how does it work for cystinuria?
faqs.answer: Tiopronin is a thiol drug that reacts with cysteine in urine to form a mixed disulfide molecule approximately 50 times more soluble than cystine itself. This reduces the concentration of free cystine available to crystallise. It is taken orally 2–3 times daily and is monitored by 24-hour urine cystine measurement.

faqs.question: How common is cystinuria?
faqs.answer: Cystinuria affects approximately 1 in 7,000 people worldwide — around 20,000 Americans and 6,000 in the UK. It is the most common single-gene disorder causing kidney stones and accounts for 1–2% of all adult kidney stones and up to 10% of childhood kidney stones.

faqs.question: Does cystinuria affect children differently than adults?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children with cystinuria often present earlier and have a higher proportion of paediatric kidney stones attributable to cystinuria (8–10%) than adults. Stone episodes in children can affect growth and school attendance, and radiation-sparing imaging (ultrasound) is prioritised. Thiol drugs require careful weight-based dosing in children.

faqs.question: What urine pH should I aim for if I have cystinuria?
faqs.answer: A urine pH of 7.0–7.5 is the target for cystinuria management. At this range, cystine solubility more than doubles compared with neutral pH. Potassium citrate is the standard medication to achieve alkalinisation. Home pH testing with strips or a pH meter allows daily titration.

faqs.question: Why does sodium intake matter in cystinuria?
faqs.answer: High dietary sodium increases renal cystine excretion by reducing the proximal tubule's capacity to reabsorb amino acids. Restricting sodium to below 2 grams per day can reduce urinary cystine output by approximately 20–30%, which is clinically meaningful in preventing crystallisation.

faqs.question: Can cystinuria be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for cystinuria currently available. The gene defect is permanent and preventive treatment must be continued lifelong. Gene therapy and novel transporter pharmacology are areas of active research. Kidney transplantation, if needed for renal failure, does not correct the genetic defect in the native kidneys.

faqs.question: What are hexagonal crystals in urine?
faqs.answer: Hexagonal crystals in urine are cystine crystals — flat, colourless, six-sided plates visible under urine microscopy. Their presence is essentially diagnostic of cystinuria. Finding them on urinalysis performed for any reason should prompt further testing including a 24-hour urine cystine measurement.

faqs.question: Can I exercise if I have cystinuria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular exercise is encouraged and has no specific restrictions. Athletes and those exercising vigorously must compensate for fluid losses by increasing intake proportionally before, during, and after exercise to maintain the dilute urine required to prevent stone formation.

faqs.question: Is cystinuria worse in men or women?
faqs.answer: Both sexes are equally likely to inherit cystinuria, but males tend to have a more severe clinical course with earlier first stone episodes and higher stone recurrence rates, possibly due to higher cystine excretion per kilogram body mass and lower habitual fluid intake in some populations.

metaDescription: Cystinuria is an inherited kidney transport defect causing recurrent cystine stones. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, hydration therapy, and surgical options.
